coredata.dc:title: A green strategy for nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots with high oxygen and chloride corrosion resistance in extremely acidic condition
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Haichao
Sun, Tian-Yu
Huang, Liang-Feng
Wei, Jiayu
Qiu, Shihui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots as a specific type of carbon material require the connection of experiment to theory, exploration of new application, and development of new material. Here we present a new nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) from 3,4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-DOPA) via the one-step synthesis of simultaneous self-polymerization and carbonization process. This water-dispersible N-PDs, composed of poly-L-DOPA aggregates and embedded nitrogen-doped graphene patches, shows the zero-dimensional structure with an average diameter of 2.06 nm. In-depth investigation of the inhibition effect of as-prepared N-PDs demonstrates that N-PDs can suppress the oxygen and chloride corrosion of mild steel in extremely acidic condition through the dual effect of adsorption/assembly onto metal surface as a passivation film and strong covalent bonding with iron, resulting in an inhibition efficiency of 97.95% at the concentration higher than 200 mg/L within 6 h, the most efficient reported for carbon dots-based corrosion inhibitors. Furthermore, density-functional-theory calculations clearly reveal that the atoms present in unsaturated bonds of N-PDs play a dominant role in strengthening the interaction between corrosion inhibitor and metal substrate, which gives rise to the stable and homogeneous adsorption of N-PDs and then the effective protection for metal substrate against corrosion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer nanodots
Adsorption
Assembly
Corrosion resistance
Extremely acidic condition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the corrosion inhibition performance of N-PDs
Exploration of the adsorption mechanism of N-PDs
Verification of the N-PDs-based passivation film on mild steel
Mechanism of the oxygen and chloride corrosion inhibition of N-PDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-PDs. (a) HAADF-STEM image, (b, c) HRTEM images, (d) statistical analysis of the size of N-PDs measured in STEM images, high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s and (g) N 1s, (h) FTIR spectra, (i) Raman spectra, (j) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, inset shows the optical image of N-PDs under 365 nm excitation (100 mg/L).
EIS plots of mild steel electrodes for the exposure to (a) 1 h, (b) 3 h and (c) 6 h in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of N-PDs.

(a) Effect of N-PDs concentrations on the impedance modulus at low frequency limit, (b) equivalent circuits used to fit the measured impedance spectra, and (c) evolution of Rf and Cf values obtained by fitting as a function of concentration.

(a) Polarization curves of mild steel electrodes treated with and without N-PDs for the exposure to 6 h in 1 M HCl, (b) Langmuir isotherm plot for the adsorption of N-PDs on mild steel surface in 1 M HCl, (c) inhibition-efficiency comparison of N-PDs with the literature values of other carbon dots, and the side and top views of the adsorption configurations of (d1) L-DOPA, (d2) polymer unit (PL), (d3) graphene patch (GP) and (d4) N-doped graphene patch (N-GP) adsorbed on Fe (110). In the top-view structures, the differential electronic densities (Δρe, isovalue 0.007 e/bohr3) are shown to reveal the microscopic bonding character, and the adsorption energies (in eV) of inhibitors also are labeled by negative Arabic numbers.

Optical micrographs and corresponding surface topographies of mild steel after 6 h exposure in 1 M HCl solution (a) without N-PDs and with (b) 10 mg/L, (c) 50 mg/L, (d) 100 mg/L, (e) 200 mg/L, (f) 300 mg/L N-PDs. (Scan region of Ra: 200 μm × 200 μm).
(a-f, a1-f1) SEM images and (a2-f2) corresponding elemental mapping images of mild steel electrodes for the exposure to 6 h in 1 M HCl solution with and without N-PDs. (Scale bar: 50 μm).

Characterization and comparison of mild steel surface for the exposure to 6 h in 1 M HCl solution with and without N-PDs. (a) Survey XPS spectra, high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) N 1s and (c) Fe 2p, and (d) Raman mappings (Scale bar: 10 μm).
Schematic illustration for oxygen and chloride corrosion inhibition of N-PDs in extremely acidic condition.
Synthetic route of nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) and its proposed structure.
Polarization parameters of electrodes treated with and without N-PDs.

head.ce:title: A green strategy for nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots with high oxygen and chloride corrosion resistance in extremely acidic condition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies
Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots as a specific type of carbon material require the connection of experiment to theory, exploration of new application, and development of new material. Here we present a new nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) from 3,4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-DOPA) via the one-step synthesis of simultaneous self-polymerization and carbonization process. This water-dispersible N-PDs, composed of poly-L-DOPA aggregates and embedded nitrogen-doped graphene patches, shows the zero-dimensional structure with an average diameter of 2.06 nm. In-depth investigation of the inhibition effect of as-prepared N-PDs demonstrates that N-PDs can suppress the oxygen and chloride corrosion of mild steel in extremely acidic condition through the dual effect of adsorption/assembly onto metal surface as a passivation film and strong covalent bonding with iron, resulting in an inhibition efficiency of 97.95% at the concentration higher than 200 mg/L within 6 h, the most efficient reported for carbon dots-based corrosion inhibitors. Furthermore, density-functional-theory calculations clearly reveal that the atoms present in unsaturated bonds of N-PDs play a dominant role in strengthening the interaction between corrosion inhibitor and metal substrate, which gives rise to the stable and homogeneous adsorption of N-PDs and then the effective protection for metal substrate against corrosion.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon material, share similar characteristics with well-known carbon nanotube, graphene, and porous/foam carbon (i.e., sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon nanostructure, top-down/bottom-up synthetic approaches, stability, and conductivity) but also have the distinct property, such as facile synthesis, high dispersity in water, surface modification flexibility, and photoluminescence, and so forth [1–3]. Despite much investigation into their preparation and application through empirical analogy, the research focused on carbon dots is in dire need of systematization for their complicated structure, innovation or diversification in characterization, and incorporation of theoretical calculation, which should further make a breakthrough in material application under the guidance of significant requirements. Besides, it’s equally important to invigorate new members and fresh ideas into their family.

Among all of these CDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) have received more attention, owing to the synthesis route parallel to graphene [4–7]. Inspired by the preparation of GQDs via “bottom-up” method [8], polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) has been designed as the precursor to successfully synthesize the conjugated polymer dots on the basis of intramolecular oxidative polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [9]. Distinct from this reaction mechanism, linear non-conjugated polymers can act as the precursor by means of mutual aggregation, assembly or cross-linking to prepare non-conjugated polymer dots (PDs) [10,11]. As a specific type of CDs, PDs reserve a part of polymer structure to the benefit of further modification. To date, a broad series of methods for obtaining PDs followed the polymerization of precursor have been developed [12–14], but high-efficiency one-step strategy for the fabrication of PDs is still a challenge in this field. Recently, it was reported that a new class of coordination polymer nanodots (CPNs) could be synthesized directly by means of the coordination between ferric ions and natural antioxidants (i.e., curcumin (Cur), quercetin (Quer), gallic acid (GA), and proanthocyanidin (PC)) [15].

Focused on the metal corrosion, chloride ion erosion occurred widely in various environments, due to its small ionic radius, strong penetrability, and powerful adsorption onto metal surface, is regarded as a risk factor that cannot be neglected. In particular, the chloride ion in acidic environment induces the formation of chloride-dominant salt layer on the steel surface, further resulting in the increased pitting. To deal with this issue, organic compounds with low molecular weight aromatic ring and long alkyl chain, serve as the eco-friendly and efficient corrosion inhibitor in place of previous molybdates, chromates and phosphates [16–18]. Besides, the electronegative heteroatoms are generally introduced into these compounds to improve their adsorption capacity onto metal surface, such as N, O, P or S atoms. Thus, carbon dots with similar carbon skeleton structure containing heteroatoms, principally nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), have been developed as the novel corrosion inhibitor [19–22]. Though relevant experimental investigation displayed promising results, the contribution of different nitrogen-doping type has been not fully understood by a shortage of detail.

In this study, we designed a green one-step synthesis route for novel nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) from the precursor 3,4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-DOPA), integrated oxidative polymerization with carbonization process. The water-dispersible N-PDs are composed of poly-L-DOPA aggregates and embedded nitrogen-doped graphene patches, and show the zero-dimensional structure with an average diameter of 2.06 nm. The investigations for the inhibition effect of N-PDs in extremely acidic condition, including corrosion inhibition performance, adsorption behavior, density-functional-theory calculations, and passivate film characterization, proved that as-prepared N-PDs are the reliable alternative for metal protection. The facile fabrication and superior structure of N-PDs pay the way for their potential applications in corrosion inhibition, such as introducing into organic coating as the additive, exfoliating, or functionalizing the layered material.

3,4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-DOPA) was supplied from Aladdin and used as received. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 ∼ 38 wt%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) were synthesized by the carbonization/self-polymerization of L-DOPA and then extraction. Briefly, 1.97 g of 3,4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-DOPA, 10 mmol) and 0.4 g of NaOH powder (10 mmol) were both dissolved into 75 mL of deionized water to obtain a clear solution and then were transferred into a 100 mL of autoclave with a Teflon liner and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. The final brown solution was dialyzed and N-PDs were collected by centrifugation and vacuum freeze-drying.

The microstructure of N-PDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (Talos F200x, ThemoFisher, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra analysis was performed on a spectrometer (NICOLET 6700, Thermo, USA) from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The compositions of specimens were determined by Raman spectroscopy (inVia Reflex, Renishaw, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK) with an Al Kα (1486.7 eV) source. The optical property of N-PDs is examined in 1 M HCl solution via the UV–vis spectrometer (Lambda 950, Perkin Elmer, USA) and fluorescence spectrometer (FL3-111, Horiba, Japan) under the excitation wavelength of 280 nm and the emission wavelength of 502 nm. All electrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemistry workstation (CHI-660E, CH Instruments, USA) equipped with a typical three-electrode device, composed of a reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode (SCE)), a counter electrode (platinum plate with 2.5 cm2 area) and a working electrode (mild steel with 1 cm × 1 cm of exposed area). To investigate their corrosion inhibition performance, N-PDs were dissolved into 1 M of HCl aqueous solution to obtain a homogeneous testing solution with various concentration. Electrochemical-impedance-spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was recorded after open circuit potential (OCP) maintained a steady state, into the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz using an alternating current signal with the amplitude of 10 mV. In addition, polarization curve experiment was collected within ± 300 mV versus OCP value with the scanning rate of 1 mV/s. Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSM 700, Zeiss, Germany) was used to examine the morphology and roughness on the steel surface. The specimen morphology after the immersion was obtained via a field-emission scanning electron microscope (Quanta FEG 250, FEI, USA) equipped with an X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer. The adsorption of N-PDs onto mild steel was characterized qualitatively by Raman mapping measurements on a confocal microscope Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia Reflex, Renishaw, UK). The selected mapping region was scanned over under an excitation wavelength of 532 nm, and full Raman spectra at all pixels were recorded and processed through removing cosmic rays, noise filtering using principal component analysis by WiRE 4.1 software.

Density-functional-theory (DFT) calculations were performed by using Quantum ESPRESSO code package [23]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) type functional (spin polarized) in the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [24] was used to describe the electronic exchange–correlation interaction. The projector augmented wave (PAW) method [25] was used to describe the electron–ion interaction. The van der Waals (vdW) interaction was considered using the DFT-D3 method with the D3 type dispersive electronic force [26]. This GGA-PBE+vdW-D3 potential is a very efficient method that has been successfully used to calculate the interfaces between transition-metal surfaces and graphene layers [27], where the interface structures and bonding energies are highly consistent (by ∼ 1 meV per C atom) with the results calculated using a very high-level (i.e., accurate and computationally expensive) DFT method (i.e., random-phase approximation). Considering the similar interface compositions (i.e., transition metal surface and carbon-based coating), this method should also be favored here. The energy cutoffs for the electronic wave functions and densities were 50 and 400 Ry, respectively. The atomic-force convergence threshold for ionic relaxation is 10−3 Ry/bohr, and the electronic-energy convergence threshold is 10−6 Ry for the self-consistent calculation of each atomic structure. To model the Fe (110) surface, a periodic slab with four atomic layers of Fe (110) with a vacuum space of ≥ 20 Å was used to separate the interaction between neighboring slab images. A periodic supercell of 3 × 3 × 1 times of the unit cell of Fe (110) was constructed to accommodate the inhibitor molecules, where the electronic-state energy levels are smeared using a Gaussian function with a width of 0.01 Ry.

Scheme 1 depicts the synthesis route of nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) from L-DOPA and the proposed structure. Similar to the mussel-inspired poly-DOPA, amorphous poly-L-DOPA can be produced from the monomer L-DOPA via the oxidation by ambient oxygen and self-polymerization under alkaline conditions [28–31]. The simultaneous solvothermal treatment plays a significant part both in the carbonization process and the formation of amide bond from free amine and carboxyl groups through the intermolecular crosslinking and folding, which contributes to the carbonization products into poly-L-DOPA aggregates to form N-PDs.

The microstructure of polymer nanodots with the influence of temperature is firstly investigated via the transmission electron microscopy, indicating that a higher carbonization temperature leads to amorphous nanoparticles with the diameter over 10 nm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). Fig. 1 a shows the scanning transmission electron microscopy in high angle annular dark field (HAADF-STEM) image of N‐PDs with uniform particle scale and dispersion without apparent aggregation. High-resolution scanning TEM (HRTEM) indicates that most particles present quasi-spherical in shape with an average diameter of 2.06 nm and obvious lattice fringes with labeled interplanar distances of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) lattice spacing of graphitic (sp2) carbon along the [001] direction (Fig. 1b–d) [32]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further performed to analyze the elemental composition and nitrogen bonding configurations in N-PDs. The high-resolution C 1s core level spectrum (Fig. 1e), is fitted with four deconvoluted peaks at binding energies of 284.6, 285.3, 288.1 and 288.8 eV, which are assigned to the carbon atoms in C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, C=O–NH and COOH chemical states, respectively [33–35]. Correspondingly the deconvoluted peaks represented the oxygen atoms in two different valence states (C–OH and C=O) appeared at binding energies of 531.2 and 531.7 eV in the high-resolution O 1s core level spectrum (Fig. 1f) [36]. The rather wide N 1s core level peak (Fig. 1g) is deconvoluted to two peaks at 398.2 and 399.7 eV, which correspond to the pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen [37]. The existence of N atoms in the form of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen results from the substitution for carbon atoms within the graphene layers and the π-conjugated system with a pair of p-electrons in the graphene layers, verified the formation of C–N bonds [38]. The Fourier-Transform-Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was applied to identify the structure of organic compounds (Fig. 1h). The –OH and C=O stretching vibrations from phenolic hydroxyl groups (Ar-OH) and carboxyl groups contributes to the characteristic absorption bands at 3300 ∼ 2500 cm−1/1406 cm−1 and 1656 cm−1/1250 cm−1 in the spectrum of L-DOPA, while the characteristic peak at 1629 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1052 cm−1 in N-PDs are both assigned to C=O stretching vibration [39]. After the carbonization and self-polymerization, the band at 3200 ∼ 2500 cm−1 almost disappears and the characteristic peak at 1570 cm−1 decays significantly in the FTIR spectrum of obtained N-PDs relative to L-DOPA, proving the consumption of –COOH groups during the formation of amide bond and the reduction of Ar-OH in N-PDs [40]. Distinguished from sharp and narrow peaks from L-DOPA, two broad bands at 1390 (D-band) and 1606 cm−1 (G-band) in the Raman spectrum of N-PDs (Fig. 1i) can be assigned to the vibration of sp3 hybrid carbon atoms in amorphous structure and sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in two-dimensional hexagonal structure, suggesting that the intercalation of N atoms into the conjugated carbon backbone has led to somewhat disordered structures [41–43]. And the relative intensity of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (ID/IG) ratio is 0.71, which may result from their oxygen-rich edges [44]. Therefore, our results suggest that the synthesized water-dispersible N-PDs, combined nitrogen-doped graphene patches with poly-L-DOPA aggregates, exhibits the carboxylate-terminated zero-dimensional particles with an average diameter of 2.06 nm, as shown in Scheme 1.

The UV–vis spectra of L-DOPA and N-PDs both displays two similar characteristic peaks centered at 220 and 278 nm, which can be ascribed to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of aromatic C=C and C=O bonds, respectively [45,46]. Meanwhile, a strong green emission band was observed at 500 nm in the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-PDs with the corresponding excitation wavelength of 280 nm (Fig. 1j). Note that the excitation spectrum of N-PDs showed the increased fluorescence intensity at 320 and 436 nm under the emission wavelength of 500 nm, combined with the fact of apparent absorption band at the wavelength region from 300 nm to 450 nm in its UV–vis spectrum, proving the success nitrogen-doping in N-PDs structure [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the corrosion inhibition performance of N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Open circuit potential (OCP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are both performed to examine the corrosion inhibitive efficiency of nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) for mild steel electrodes in 1 M HCl. As shown in Table S1, though the negligible fluctuation in the OCP values for specimens with the immersion time, the potential didn’t slightly shift towards a negative value until the concentration of N-PDs above 100 mg/L. EIS data of mild steel electrodes treated by the monomer L-DOPA in acid condition showed that the additive of L-DOPA results in no significant improvement in corrosion inhibition compared to that in only acid condition (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). It’s a certainty that a progressive breakdown appears in bare mild steel electrode with the extended exposure to HCl solution, while the treatment of N-PDs results in the extended capacitive loop, heightened impedance modulus and phase angle. In Fig. 2 a1-c1, Nyquist plots of mild steel electrodes treated with and without N-PDs both showed the depressed semicircle capacitive loop at main frequencies and inductive loop at low frequencies. And the diameter of capacitive loop increased with the concentration of N-PDs, suggesting that N-PDs control the mild steel corrosion mainly through the charge transfer process during the metal dissolution [47]. In Bode impedance modulus plots, the impedance modulus at low frequency limit |Z|0.01 Hz and the peak value of phase angle displayed a definite enhancement with the addition of corrosion inhibitors, and the increment of both of EIS parameters leveled off with the concentration of N-PDs over 50 mg/L. The increased impedance modulus shown in Fig. 2a2–c2 directly suggests that adsorbed N-PDs contribute to the formation of inhibitors film and thereby passivate the metal surface [48]. In Bode phase angle plots, the peak of phase angle shifted to the higher frequencies with the concentration of N-PDs increasing (Fig. 2a3-2c3). Furthermore, the phase angle of mild steel electrodes after 6 h exposure increased from −25.7° (0 mg/L) to the maximum −71.2° (300 mg/L) closer towards −90°, indicating the capacitive behavior has an effect on smoothening the metal surface, thereby inhibiting metal corrosion [49].

According to the above impedance diagrams, the impedance modulus at low frequency limit |Z|0.01 Hz and its growth rate are summarized in Fig. 3 a to examine the corrosion behavior of mild steel with the absence and presence of N-PDs. In contrast to the bare mild steel, the evolution of |Z|0.01 Hz of mild steel treated by HCl solution with N-PDs exhibits a converse increase trend with the extended exposure, revealed the effective inhibition of N-PDs against the metal corrosion. Note that the growth of |Z|0.01 Hz with high concentration of N-PDs over 50 mg/L scales linearly with the treatment time, distinct from that in 10 mg/L and 50 mg/L. This implies that N-PDs provides the sufficient quantity for metal protection in this circumstance at concentrations of higher than 50 mg/L.

A further curve fitting analysis for the measured impedance spectra is applied on the basis of equivalent circuits in Fig. 3b. Given the depressed capacitive loop in Nyquist plots as a result of the roughness and non-uniformity of steel surface, constant phase element (CPE) is used in place of capacitance to achieve more accurate results, which is defined as [50]:
(1)
Z
C P E
=
Y 0
- 1
j ω
- n
where Y0, j, ω and n represent the CPE constant (Ω cm−2 s−n), imaginary number (j2 = −1), angular frequency (rad s−1) and exponent, respectively.

In the equivalent circuit (I), the charge transfer resistance Rct is connected with corresponding double-layer capacitance CPEdl and the inductive resistance/inductance RLL in parallel, and finally placed in series to the solution resistance Rs. This equivalent circuit (I) is applicable for bare mild steel immersed in acid solution, where the appearance of inductive loop can be ascribed to the relaxation process of surface adsorption and coverage of intermediate such as
C l
a d s
-
and
H
a d s
+
onto electro-negative steel [51,52]. Equivalent circuit (II) is used in the presence of N-PDs, which film resistance Rf and corresponding film capacitance CPEf are introduced in place of Rct and CPEdl. This behavior describes the adsorption and assembly of N-PDs as the dominant species at the solution/steel interface instead of charge transfer process. And in this case, the inductive loop corresponds to the reversible adsorption–desorption process of corrosion inhibitor at the passivation film surface [53]. The fitting curves and whole fitting parameters obtained from fitting by equivalent circuits are shown in Fig. 2a-2c and Table S2, respectively. It can be seen that the equivalent circuit is a good fit for experimental data for most of frequency range and gives small value of the goodness of fit (χ2) within 0.01, confirming its validity. The exponent n, relative to the surface roughness, can be combined with resistance to characterize CPE as double-layer capacitance Cdl (μF cm−2) or film capacitance Cf (μF cm−2), using the equation as given [54,55]:
(2)
C
d l
=
Y 0
1 / n
1
R s
+
1
R
c t
( n - 1 ) / n
(3)
C f
=
Y 0
R f
1 - n
1 / n

First of all, the solution resistance Rs, represented the conductivity of solution, decreased apparently with immersion time in the presence of N-PDs, which is ascribed to the consumption of inhibitor in solution. Rf and calculated Cf correspond to the resistance and theoretical capacitance values of passivation film, described its dielectric property. As a consequence of the accumulation of passivation film hindering the metal corrosion, Rf value increased with N-PDs concentration and immersion time, while the evolution of Cf displayed an opposite trend (Fig. 3c). The fact that the adsorption of N-PDs removes water with higher dielectric constant absorbed on the metal surface results in the significant drop of Cf at the low concentration of N-PDs relative to control specimen. Nevertheless, Cf value tended to level off with time during the sustained growth of Rf at the concentration higher than 50 mg/L, suggesting that the reversible exchange of N-PDs assembled on steel surface with those in acid condition cannot be neglected though the thickened passivation film suppresses the electrolyte uptake and patulous capillary channels [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the adsorption mechanism of N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Polarization measurement, a well-explored approach to investigation for the kinetics of anodic/cathodic reactions and inhibitive mechanism of N-PDs, is depicted in Fig. 4 a and the polarization parameters acquired by polarization curves using Tafel extrapolation method are itemized in Table 1 . The surface coverage θ and inhibition efficiency were calculated by corrosion current density icorr using the following equation:
(4)
θ =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
i
c o r r
0
(5)
I n h i b i t i o n
e f f i c i e n c y =
i
c o r r
0
-
i
corr
i
c o r r
0
× 100 %
where
i
c o r r
0
and
i
c o r r
represent the corrosion current density with the absence and presence of N-PDs, respectively. Clearly both the anodic and cathodic curves shift to the values of lower current densities with the concentration of N-PDs, suggesting that this corrosion inhibitor plays a suppressed part in both anodic and cathodic corrosion reactions. Compared to the control specimen, the corrosion potential Ecorr moved in positive orientation with the low concentration of N-PDs but shifted toward less negative values at the concentration higher than 50 mg/L. Owing to the maximum displacement of Ecorr less than 85 mV, N-PDs is considered to be a mixed-typed corrosion inhibitor [57]. Furthermore, the presence of passive region on anodic branch for the concentration over 50 mg/L suggests the predominant inhibition effect of N-PDs versus the anodic reaction, which is further confirmed by the significant change for anode slope βa relative to cathode slope βc (Table 1). The corrosion current density icorr corresponds to the corrosion rate, and decreases with the concentration of N-PDs, indicating its effective protection against corrosion.

Additionally, the adsorption process is further analyzed to investigate the interaction between N-PDs and metal surface, by means of fitting the surface coverage θ at various concentrations to different adsorption isotherms, e.g., Langmuir, Frumkin, Temkin, Flory-Huggins, Dhar-Flory-Huggins, and Bockris-Swinkels models [58]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the optimized correlation between experimental results and isotherm functions was obtained from the Langmuir adsorption isotherm with the correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9998, interpreted by the following equation [59]:
(6)
c
i n h
θ
=
1
K
a d s
+
c
i n h
where θ and cinh represent surface coverage and inhibitor concentration, respectively. Kads is the equilibrium constant of N-PDs adsorption process, and then can be used to calculate the value of corresponding standard free energy of adsorption
G
a d s
0
, determined by the equation as given:
(7)
G
a d s
0
= - R T l n
(
c
H 2
O
K
a d s
)

The Kads and
G
a d s
0
value of N-PDs adsorption onto mild steel in 1 M HCl solution at room temperature are calculated as 0.2125 mg−1 L and −30.41 kJ mol−1, suggesting the spontaneity of adsorption process and the stability of adsorbed layer. This fact also implies that N-PDs is adsorbed on metal surface both via physisorption (e.g., electrostatic and van der Waals interactions) and chemisorption (electron sharing and transferring between Lewis basic sites of inhibitor and vacant d orbitals of metal surface) [60–62].

Fig. 4c depicts the corrosion inhibition of N-PDs calculated by the Equation (5) at various concentration. The corrosion inhibition capacity of N-PDs, with an inhibition efficiency above 97 % at the concentration higher than 200 mg/L within 6 h, is the optimal one so far based on a review of the literature focused on various carbon quantum dots in terms of treatment time, inhibitor concentration, and inhibition efficiency. Details of the conditions for the corrosion inhibition experiments (treatment time, inhibitor concentration, and inhibition efficiency) are given in Table S3.

On the basis of electrochemical measurement, it can be proved that the impermeability of N-PDs takes the positive effect on its anti-corrosion performance. This effective impermeability depends on the abundant aromatic ring in N-PDs with intrinsic “pore” size (∼0.06 nm) smaller than most of atoms and molecules, as well as the coverage of N-PDs onto steel surface affected by the concentration. As another dominant factor for corrosion resistance, the adsorption strength corresponds to the anchoring property of corrosion inhibitor onto metal substrate, which is hardly detected by experimental methods, and molecular simulation is needed to investigate the binding strength of N-PDs onto metal. To further reveal the microscopic adsorption behavior of N-PDs on steel surface, as well as the underlying adsorption mechanisms, density-functional-theory (DFT) calculations are performed for N-PDs and L-DOPA for comparison. According to the experimental FTIR and XPS results, the N-PDs, comprised of self-polymerization and carbonization products from L-DOPA, possesses the abundant pyrrolic nitrogen, graphene patch, and pyridinic nitrogen. On the basis of the above, three unit-geometry structures are used to represent N-PDs, i.e., the unit of L-DOPA self-polymerized product (PL), graphene patch (GP), and N-doping graphene patch (N-GP). Fig. 4d1-4d4 show the optimized configurations of L-DOPA, PL, GP, and N-GP units adsorbed on the Fe surface, as well as the corresponding differential electronic densities (Δρe) caused by the adsorption interaction. All of the adsorbates exhibit the lying gestures as their most favored configurations on Fe surface, which can effectively reduce the exposed area of Fe surface and prevent the corrosion media from contacting the metal surface. The adsorption stability of these inhibitors can be quantitatively described by their adsorption energies E ad, expressed as below:
(8)
E
a d
=
E
X @ F e
-
(
E X
+
E
F e
)
where E X@Fe, E X, and E Fe represent the electronic energies of an inhibitor unit on Fe, an isolated inhibitor molecule, and the clean Fe substrate, respectively. The definition of E ad in Equation (8) indicates that an exothermic adsorption contributes to a negative value of E ad, which decreases with the increase of inhibitor adsorption stability. The inhibitor units PL, GP and N-GP from N-PDs have their E ad values of −5.37, −4.95 and −4.47 eV, lower than that of L-DOPA (E ad = −2.73 eV), namely, N-PDs show a significantly stronger interaction with Fe substrate than L-DOPA. To understand the interaction mechanism between inhibitors and Fe surface, the Δρe distributions are analyzed to reveal the covalent bonding and electronic transfer between inhibitors and Fe surface. From the Δρe plots in Fig. 4d, the atoms in the presence of unsaturated bonds of inhibitors (e.g., C and O atoms in the C=C and C=O bonds) are inclined to accumulate the electrons transferred from the Fe surface, and form effective covalent bonds with Fe surface, which will be clearly proved by XPS results in the following. As a consequence, owing to the existence of more hexagon/pentagon atomic rings, the planar inhibitor units in N-PDs (e.g., the PL, GP and N-GP units considered here) show stronger interaction with Fe surface than the distorted (much less planar) L-DOPA unit with only one hexagon atomic ring. In addition, the planar inhibitor units also have larger contact areas with the metal substrate than that of L-DOPA. These calculated energetic and geometric results confirm that more unsaturated bonds and aromatic rings have endowed N-PDs with a stronger interaction with Fe substrate and more superior corrosion inhibition effect relative to L-DOPA. The atomic-scale chemical understanding established here by DFT calculation not only reveals that the relatively high content of C=C in N-PDs is important for their spontaneous and stable adsorption, but also indicates that it is an effective way to obtain the desired inhibitor-metal interaction strength by controlling the atomic-bond states in the N-PDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the N-PDs-based passivation film on mild steel
ce:section.ce:para: The visualization and verification of adsorption effect are performed via the surface topography and microstructure characterization on mild steel in the absence and presence of N-PDs. As the control specimen, optical micrograph of bare steel treated in 1 M HCl solution displayed a dense distribution of a large number of annular corrosion pits on the metal surface with etched grain boundaries, indicating its severe destruction without omission (Fig. 5 a). With the increase of inhibitor concentration, the scattered corrosion pits on the steel surface tend to reduce in density, even almost disappear at the concentration higher than 50 mg/L, which implied the effective inhibition of N-PDs (Fig. 5b-5f). Correspondingly, the homogeneous adsorption and assembly of N-PDs contribute to the smooth metal surface with visible grain boundaries, as proven by the lower average roughness Ra in the presence of N-PDs than control specimen (Fig. 5a1-5f1). In addition, it also is the strong inhibitor-substrate interaction as revealed by above DFT calculations that leads to such homogeneous adsorption and assembly of N-PDs.

Scanning electronic microscope (SEM) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) is used to investigate the surface morphology and further determine the corrosion inhibition ability of N-PDs. Fig. 6 a shows the typical morphology of mild steel immersed in HCl solution, with the distinct erosion marks (i.e., uneven surface and accumulated corrosion product) in relative to untreated bare mild steel (Fig. S4, Supporting Information) [63]. Identified by EDS signal, Cl element was enriched around the raised corroded region (Point 1, Fig. 6a1-6a2). As shown in Fig. 6b, the raised corroded region was disorganized partially on metal surface treated by N-PDs of 10 mg/L, though the content of Cl wasn’t reduced. Fig. 6e-6f depicts the similar morphology of smooth surface with minor abrasive scratches but a few accumulated corrosion particles, which is different from the steel treated with the N-PDs concentration above 100 mg/L. In terms of EDS analysis, the presence of lower amounts of oxygen and chloride than that of control specimen suggests that N-PDs play a suppressive part in the removal of adsorbed O and Cl atoms onto metal surface.

XPS analysis is performed to compare the elemental composition on mild steel without and with the treatment of N-PDs. Survey spectrum on mild steel without N-PD treatment shows the emerging Cl 2s peak, implying the enriched chloride adsorption onto surface (Fig. 7 a). According to the N 1s spectra (Fig. 7b), the deconvoluted peak centered at 398.7 eV is typical of −Fe−N− bond as a result of adsorption of N-PDs, strongly supporting the covalent bonding between unsaturated nitrogen atoms and Fe surface proposed above based on the DFT calculation results. Besides, the doublet peak described in Fe 2p spectra (Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2) shifts simultaneously toward the higher binding energies relative to the control specimen, which is ascribed to the transformation of electrons from iron atom to the stronger electronegative nitrogen atom (Fig. 7c), consistent with the DFT calculation results above. As a fundamental technique to identify functional groups in organic molecules, Raman spectroscopy has been combined with microscopy to mapping out the specific Raman signal and further characterize N-PDs-based passivation film on steel substrate. Fig. 7d displays the spatial distribution of integrated intensity around 1606 cm−1, which corresponds to sp2-hybrided carbon atoms in N-PDs. As a result of adsorption of N-PDs, the average Raman intensity increased by an order of magnitude (at the concentration of 10 mg/L), even by two orders of magnitude with the concentration higher than 50 mg/L relative to control group. Besides, the homogeneous adsorption and assembly of N-PDs results in a slight fluctuation of integrated intensity with the concentration of 100 mg/L (Fig. 7d4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the oxygen and chloride corrosion inhibition of N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: A rational conception of the oxygen and chloride corrosion inhibition of N-PDs in extremely acidic condition is proposed in Fig. 8 . Nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs) exhibits the regular zero-dimensional structure combined nitrogen-doped graphene patches with poly-L-DOPA aggregates. Owing to the nanoscale effect, the stable N-PDs in acid solution are inclined to adsorb onto metal surface and form a passivation film through the assembly of each other, which can reduce the exposed area of metal surface in corrosion media. On the one hand, the negatively charged carboxyl groups enriched on the passivation layer surface play a significant part in hindering the same charged corrosion media. On the other hand, a large number of pyrrolic nitrogen atoms anchor onto Fe surface via the coordination bond so that N-PDs adsorption layer shows the strengthened interaction with metal substrate, associated with the polymer chains arranged in parallel to prevent corrosion media (i.e., O2, H2O, and Cl−) from contacting the metal surface. In comparison with the previously reported corrosion inhibitors, N-PDs provide an additional barrier effect from the embedded nitrogen-doped graphene patches for preventing the permeation of corrosion media. Therefore, driven by the above comprehensive feature, N-PDs can effectively suppress the oxygen and chloride corrosion, even in the extremely acidic condition accompanied by severe active corrosion.

In summary, a green synthesis route for simultaneous self-polymerization and carbonization of 3,4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-DOPA) was firstly developed to prepare the nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots (N-PDs). Morphology and characterization confirmed that the water-dispersible N-PDs composed of poly-L-DOPA aggregates and embedded nitrogen-doped graphene patches, show the zero-dimensional structure with an average diameter of 2.06 nm. In-depth understanding of the inhibition effect of as-prepared N-PDs, including corrosion inhibition performance, adsorption behavior, and passivate film characterization, is investigated and discussed in detail. Besides, density-functional-theory (DFT) calculation demonstrates convincingly that the atoms in the presence of unsaturated bonds of N-PDs (e.g., C and O atoms in the C=C and C=O bonds) play a dominant role in strengthening the interaction between corrosion inhibitor and metal substrate to achieve effective protection, which has guidance significance to the anticorrosion studies for carbon dots. This study showed that as-prepared N-PDs are the reliable alternative for metal protection in the extreme environment, thereby significantly broadening the specific applications of nanodots. This novel polymer nanodot provides an excellent functional material candidate for efficient applications in the field of nanomedicine, imaging, sensors, and nanoprobe etc.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely appreciate the technical support by Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.135242.

ce:section.ce:para:
